title: Peregrino
anchor_summary: Isometric survival-management road journey with party care, caravan upgrading, scarce-resource decision-making, and horror threats during exploration.

similar_games.title: Wartales
similar_games.why_similar: Open-ended isometric party survival with camp upkeep, resource pressure, equipment progression, and constant travel across a hostile world.

similar_games.title: This War of Mine
similar_games.why_similar: Survival management built around scavenging runs, shelter upkeep, companion needs, and harsh life-or-death choices under persistent scarcity.

similar_games.title: Darkest Dungeon II
similar_games.why_similar: Bleak run-based journey structure centered on moving between encounters, managing a fragile party, and enduring horror-driven attrition.

similar_games.title: Vagrus - The Riven Realms
similar_games.why_similar: Caravan-focused expedition game with crew management, supplies, travel risk, and difficult route-planning across a punishing setting.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Peregrino as an isometric survival management game centered on crossing a cursed land, scavenging, hunting, fighting, managing scarce resources, inventory pressure, nightfall danger, and extracting with loot. The horror identity is strong via grotesque abominations, cursed woods, and survival-focused threat escalation after dusk. The game also has clear party/caravan management elements: caring for companions, leading pilgrims, making fate-affecting decisions, and upgrading caravan crafting stations. That makes it broader than pure survival horror, so survival_horror fits best as the primary archetype with tactical_rpg and party_based_crpg as secondary influences rather than full identities.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a short, single-player scrapbook puzzler built around sorting mementos, reassembling torn photos, and solving gentle logic/word puzzles across handcrafted pages while uncovering an authored story about friendship and aging. There is no combat, the tone is explicitly cozy and emotional, and interaction appears cursor-driven through scrapbook pages. While the copy does not strongly emphasize classic hidden-object searching, the closest supported taxonomy fit is hidden_object_puzzle because the core loop is light object-focused puzzle interaction in handcrafted scenes/pages with narrative progression.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence consistently describes Fortuna Magus as a fantasy JRPG with a rich authored story, single-player campaign structure, and strategic turn-based party battles. Core gameplay details are concrete and repeated across Steam short/detailed descriptions and Metacritic: characters pursue a missing-father narrative, battles revolve around Revelations, TP management, tandem attacks, and learning new spells/skills through combat conditions and element thresholds. That supports a story-led JRPG identity with party management, magic-focused turn-based combat, quest-driven progression, and skill customization. Alternative candidates like turn_based_tactics are weaker because there is no evidence of grid tactics or overhead tactical structure, while monster_collect_rpg lacks creature-collection evidence.

similar_games.why_similar: Casual co-op physics chaos with players manipulating furniture and wrecking orderly rooms during short missions.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes a cute multiplayer pet-chaos game with co-op, PvP, and asymmetric roles (pets cause destruction, owner catches/defends), plus collectibles and a home-building mode. However, the material does not clearly anchor the game to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The strongest signals are party/co-op/social chaos, but the evidence does not concretely define a match-based party-game structure, nor does it support action-adventure, platformer, shooter, racer, sim, or RPG archetypes in the allowed list. The current taxonomy audit already flagged a provider gap with no candidate archetypes. Because the gameplay description is broad, mode-mixed, and not strongly aligned to a supported archetype, classification should be rejected for now.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Banquet for Fools as a single-player, open-world, party-based RPG centered on investigating a village disappearance across the island of Invimona. Core loop evidence is strong: exploring towns and holds for clues, taking quests/requests, managing four companions with stat-driven dialogue options and bonding, and progressing characters through skills, spells, songs, equipment, and level-ups. Combat is explicitly described as brawl-style beat-'em-up action with pausable party command decisions, leader switching, support combos, and coordinated attacks, supporting a western_narrative_rpg classification with beat_em_up as a secondary flavor rather than the primary identity. The mystery-driven quest structure, party management, authored campaign framing, and open-world exploration are all directly evidenced in the Steam detailed description and echoed by the short description/Metacritic copy.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cupiclaw as a 'cute & frantic claw machine roguelike deckbuilder' centered on grabbing prizes, building combos, customizing runs, upgrading the claw, and facing increasingly chaotic machines. That is enough to establish a clear gameplay identity, but the identity does not cleanly map to any allowed archetype in the taxonomy. It is not supported as sports_sim, realistic_racer, or pinball despite the current computed near-misses, and it also does not have sufficient evidence of card-battle combat to justify card_battler. Because the core loop appears to be an arcade claw-machine roguelike/deckbuilding hybrid outside the available archetype set, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a misleading fit.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Physics-driven roguelike run structure where shots produce item synergies, upgrades, and combo-heavy board interactions.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/metadata evidence consistently describes Bulb Boy 2 as a single-player point-and-click horror adventure built around solving tricky puzzles, navigating eerie handcrafted spaces, and unfolding a linear twisted story. Core signals are strong for cursor-driven interaction, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear progression, mystery/survival-escape framing, and essentially non-combat-first play. The text does mention facing jelly monsters and a boss, so I treated combat as light rather than none, but the dominant gameplay identity still aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle / point-and-click puzzle adventure. Horror tone is strong but not enough to override the puzzle-first structure as the primary archetype.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Docked as a realistic port-operations simulator centered on operating heavy machinery, completing mission-based jobs and contracts, repairing equipment, expanding infrastructure, purchasing lots and machinery, and growing a logistics business. The current computed taxonomy over-indexes on racing because of vehicle language, but the actual gameplay loop is commercial cargo handling and operational expansion, not racing competition.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present the game as a shop/store management sandbox first: designing the shop layout, organizing shelves, setting prices, managing staff, crafting stock and upgrades, and building barricades/traps. That strongly fits management_tycoon with systemic sandbox/base growth. The zombie defense layer is significant but appears additive to the management loop rather than the main identity; combat is described as defending the shop during raids and hordes, not as the core session structure. Colony_sim and city_builder are nearby matches because of survivor assignment, construction, and base defense, but the clearest central fantasy is operating and optimizing a business in an apocalypse.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies the game as a roguelite tower defense: placing towers, walls, and traps, shaping enemy paths, surviving wave-based encounters, drafting Boon Cards, fighting boss enemies each run, and unlocking permanent upgrades between runs. That is strong evidence for the game's gameplay identity, but the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 does not include a tower defense / tower-defense roguelite archetype. Available archetypes such as rts, turn_based_tactics, card_battler, or co_op_action_roguelite would misrepresent the core play loop. Because the evidence is strong yet does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

The core loop also includes procedurally generated dungeon runs, loot/material collection, and village rebuilding with facilities, crops, cooking, and skill-tree growth, which supports roguelite metaprogression as a secondary identity rather than displacing the metroidvania core. Up to 4-player co-op is explicit, but the strongest gameplay identity remains exploration-driven action-platforming with ability-gated progression.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Avenue Escape as a level-based traffic-management puzzle game: 50 stages, directing cars through intersections, timing moves, avoiding accidents, and optimizing clears. That supports a puzzle/logic identity, but the available taxonomy archetype list does not include a general traffic or route-planning puzzle category. The game does not strongly fit hidden_object_puzzle, word_puzzle_strategy, pinball, rhythm_game, transport_sim, or other listed archetypes without forcing a mismatch.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Also centers on managing city traffic flow, timing signals, and guiding cars and pedestrians safely through busy intersections.
Traffic puzzle game built around planning routes and timing multiple vehicles through a city without collisions.
A road-traffic puzzle about optimizing vehicle movement through urban layouts and preventing jams through careful planning.
Shares the route-management, timing, and crash-avoidance puzzle loop, though applied to rail networks instead of cars.
Another timing-heavy transport puzzle where you control movement and signals to avoid accidents and clear increasingly tricky stages.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions and labels consistently identify this as a realistic baseball simulation. Official copy repeatedly emphasizes 'real baseball,' lifelike stadium/player presentation, exhibition play, online matches, rankings, and team-building via TEAM EDIT with roster selection and tournament regulations. Source labels include 'Baseball Sim' and baseball theme, while Steam categories support single-player, PvP, co-op, and split-screen/social play. The gameplay loop is match-based competition with realistic sports simulation rather than party-game framing.

title: Homura Hime

similar_games.why_similar: Blends fast 3D melee-and-ranged action with dense projectile dodging and stylish boss encounters.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store copy consistently describes an anime-style 3D action game centered on combo attacks, dodging/parrying, mixed close-range and ranged offense, and surviving bullet-hell projectile patterns against powerful demon-girl bosses. The emphasis on fast combo combat and boss-focused encounters strongly fits character_action. Campaign/story framing, single-player mode, weapon/equipment unlocks, and authored narrative further support this reading. No evidence points more strongly to soulslike, shooter, horror, or RPG-first structures.
